You might be involved with a parent more than usual. Many of you will explore redecorating ideas or plans for renovations. Stock the refrigerator.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>The pace of your days will accelerate for the next month because you have places to go, people to see and things to do. Short trips, increased errands and appointments, plus reading and writing will give you a busy schedule. This can support success in your job.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>"Show me the money!" A handful of planets are currently in your money house, encouraging you to explore ways to boost your income. You will also give further thought to your possessions, your assets and, at a deeper level, your values. In other words, what really matters?</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Filmmaker Spike Lee (1957) shares your birthday. You are multitalented, imaginative and tenacious. You're an optimist who radiates enthusiasm. This is a year of service, perhaps to family. Take care of yourself so you can support those who need guidance, comfort and assistance. Personalize your home. Might it be time for a makeover?</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: David Mirkovic leads No. 3 Illinois to 105-70 rout of No. 14 Penn
entries.summary: <p>GREENVILLE, S.C. — David Mirkovic had 29 points and 17 rebounds, and No. 3 seed Illinois dominated No. 14 seed Penn 105-70 on Thursday night in the first round of the NCAA Tournament.</p><p>Keaton Wagler added 18 points, seven assists and seven rebounds for the Fighting Illini (25-8), who moved on to face No. 11 VCU in the second round on Saturday.</p><p>Kylan Boswell had 13 points, while Ben Humrichous and Tomislav Ivisic each chipped in with 12 as Illinois shot 50% from the field, made 15 3-pointers and outrebounded the much smaller Quakers 48-25.</p><p>Michael Zanoni finished with 20 points on 9-of-18 shooting for Penn (18-12), the Ivy League champion.</p><p>TJ Power, who scored 44 points in the Quakers' overtime win over Yale that got them into the tournament, finished with six points on 2-of-8 shooting in 30 minutes after missing the previous two practices with an illness that left his status for the game uncertain.</p><p>Illinois coach Brad Underwood said Wagler did a “phenomenal" job guarding Power.</p><p>“He gave up the last 3 before the end of the half (to Power), which I wasn’t very happy with, but his touches were very hard,” Underwood said.</p><p>Mirkovic set the tone for Illinois with a huge first half, as his 17 points and 10 rebounds helped the Fighting Illini build a 40-30 lead.</p><p>“He was the best player on the court tonight, especially in the first half, his physicality,” Underwood said. “Ten rebounds in a half and 17 points. Again, we talk about (rebounds) being our daily vitamins and we had a 28-14 advantage at half. Then it was just delivering body blows.”</p><p>Illinois continued to grab rebound after rebound and stretched the lead to 23 with 10 minutes remaining after Mirkovic came up with a steal and fed Zvonimir Ivisic for a transition alley-oop dunk.</p><p>“Their size was tough to contend with,” Penn guard Cam Thrower said.</p><p>Mirkovic's 17 rebounds where a school tournament record. He said the ball was finding him like a magnet on the offensive glass.</p><p>“That’s what I do every day, even practices,” Mirkovic said. “I just crash the board trying to bring that physicality. I was crashing and I got a lot of easy points on the rim.”</p><p><b>Wagler: Mirkovic is nation's most overlooked freshman</b></p><p>There has been plenty made about the talent of this year's freshman class that includes Duke's Cameron Boozer, Kansas' Darryn Peterson and BYU's AJ Dybantsa.</p><p>Wagler, a second-team Associated Press All-American as a freshman, believes Mirkovic deserves to be mentioned in that class.</p><p>“All year he’s been one of the best freshmen in the country,” Wagler said. “With this loaded freshman class we have, he’s been overshadowed a bit. He rebounds, plays physical, plays hard all the time. I think tonight he just showed what he’s capable of.”</p><p><b>Fran McCaffrey said AJ Power wasn't 100% healthy</b></p><p>Penn coach Fran McCaffrey said Power had “four of five IVs” before the game but simply “wasn't himself” after battling an illness.</p><p>But he credited him for battling through and playing the entire first half.</p><p>“That is one tough kid there,” McCaffrey said. “I love him. He tried. He was sick for two days. We thought he was going to get better. ... But you could tell, in particular on the glass, because he’s an elite rebounder, that he didn’t have quite the energy or the explosion that he normally does."</p><p><b>Penn players say thank you to followers</b></p><p>After the game the Penn players walked in unison over toward their fans and applauded them. Some tugged at their jerseys and waved and others blew kisses. The fans applauded and cheered back, with one yelling, “Next year, boys!”</p><p><b>Up next</b></p><p>Illinois beat VCU 62-46 in the only meeting between the schools in 2016.<br></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Women’s NCAA brackets in Sun-Times deserve same presentation as men's
entries.summary: <p>In Sunday’s sports section, I found it dismaying to encounter the men’s and women’s NCAA brackets on facing pages: the men’s in bold, dynamic color and the women’s diminished in sad gray.</p><p>In these times when the differences between men and women, white and nonwhite, majority and minority are so amplified, equal treatment of the brackets should have been a no-brainer.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-floatRight> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c80000" name="image-c80000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b0c7a7e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/960x539+0+59/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F91%2Fe4%2F4dd8779d4e8193ff2c9520150d67%2Fimg-5612.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6fd6ff9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/960x539+0+59/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F91%2Fe4%2F4dd8779d4e8193ff2c9520150d67%2Fimg-5612.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c597b88/2147483647/strip/true/crop/960x539+0+59/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F91%2Fe4%2F4dd8779d4e8193ff2c9520150d67%2Fimg-5612.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A tabloid style newspaper opened. Two pages can be seen with chartlight graphics. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c597b88/2147483647/strip/true/crop/960x539+0+59/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F91%2Fe4%2F4dd8779d4e8193ff2c9520150d67%2Fimg-5612.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/df5e01b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/960x539+0+59/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F91%2Fe4%2F4dd8779d4e8193ff2c9520150d67%2Fimg-5612.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c597b88/2147483647/strip/true/crop/960x539+0+59/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F91%2Fe4%2F4dd8779d4e8193ff2c9520150d67%2Fimg-5612.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The men’s and women’s NCAA brackets on facing pages in Sunday’s Sun-Times.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>I understand the pages of the paper alternate between color and black and white. You can’t change that, and at least both brackets were the same size. It’s clear no one meant any insult. It was just a practical business decision based on the pages and inks available. However, all we have to do is turn back to Scoop Jackson’s column that same day to see <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/sports/2026/03/14/wbc-world-baseball-classic-team-usa-black-players-aaron-judge-byron-buxton-mark-derosa" >business decisions</a> used as license to discriminate in sports.</p><p>We can do better. There was no way to render the women’s bracket in color because it was on a page printed with only black ink. But there’s nothing that says the men’s bracket had to use colors of ink available on its page. Next time, send cyan, magenta and yellow to the bench in the name of equality.</p><p><i>Todd Schultz, St. Joseph, Michigan</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-980000" name="module-980000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3>Misplaced priorities in the U.S. </h3><h3></h3><p>WBEZ's Chip Mitchell's story of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2026/03/16/legion-park-chicago-tent-encampment-homeless-unhoused-fire" >Carmen Laude</a> in Tuesday’s Sun-Times was tragic. How can we be the richest country in the world and have people who worked lose their home over medical debt?</p><p>The story also made me break out my calculator. If we took the <a class="Link" href="https://time.com/article/2026/03/16/what-us-spending-on-the-war-in-iran-could-fund-instead/" target="_blank" >$1 billion a day</a> we are spending doing whatever it is we are doing in Iran and distributed it to our nation’s <a class="Link" href="https://endhomelessness.org/state-of-homelessness/" target="_blank" >770,000 homeless</a>, we could pay them in a day $1,300 — what it takes them to have a home for a month.</p><p>Our priorities in this country are wack. The next time someone tells you we are a Christian nation, I suggest you laugh in their face.</p><p><i>Don Anderson, Oak Park</i> </p><h3>Haunting thoughts </h3><p>I don't believe in ghosts, but it seems the Jeffrey Epstein haunting is so formidable, it compelled us to engage in a protracted war.</p><p><i>Larry Niemi, Loop</i></p><h3>Bone to pick </h3> <p>President Donald Trump has said that the Iran war will be over <a class="Link" href="https://www.politico.com/news/2026/03/13/trump-iran-war-ending-timeline-00828138" target="_blank" >“when I feel it in my bones.”</a> Does this include his <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2017/10/23/18331262/mccain-issues-veiled-criticism-of-trump-s-vietnam-deferment" >bone spurs</a> that prevented him from serving [in the military]?</p><p><i>Dennis Fitzgerald, Melbourne, Australia</i></p><h3>Next steps?</h3><p>As Donald J. Trump has done all his life, will he walk away from the U. S. presidency as he is prone to do in any situation where he does not get his way?</p><p><i>Eileen Hughes, Near North Side</i> <br></p>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: Dear Abby: Parents continue to ignore older son's request
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I am in my late 30s. I live halfway across the country from my parents and don't have the best relationship with them. I also have a brother four years younger from whom I have been estranged for 20 years. He lives in the same city as my parents.</p><p>My problem is, if I visit, my parents (especially my mom) will tell my brother and invite him over. I don't like my brother and don't want to see him. I will not reconcile with him because he did some horrible things to me 20 years ago for which I can never forgive him.</p><p>With my parents, things don't get through to them unless I take drastic measures. How can I convey my desire not to see him? My plan would be to tell them they must agree not to invite him to their house while I'm there — and if they can't respect my wishes, I simply won't see them.</p><p>I'm not worried about being without lodging because I have friends I can stay with there. In fact, I have made several trips to that city without visiting my parents. What is your perspective on how I can "lay down the law" and enforce it? — <i>INDEPENDENT IN MASSACHUSETTS</i></p><p><b>DEAR INDEPENDENT:</b> Have an honest conversation with your parents. Explain what happened between you and your brother. Emphasize that you do not wish to see him again — ever. Tell them you would like to visit them, but you do not want your brother to be informed or invited over while you are there. If they cannot agree, explain (without hostility) that you will skip the visit. This is not "laying down the law"; it is creating a boundary.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My wife and I welcomed our third child into the world six months ago. Since then, my wife has been diagnosed with postpartum depression and has cheated on me multiple times. She has now moved out. She is dating a woman and wants a divorce.</p><p>Despite all of this, I have forgiven her, and I still love her. Her behavior is negatively affecting our other children, but she refuses to acknowledge its impact and won't listen to me or talk to me about it. She refuses to go to counseling and acts like everything is my fault. How should I handle this? — <i>DISTRAUGHT HUSBAND IN PENNSYLVANIA</i></p><p><b>DEAR HUSBAND:</b> If you had no hint that your wife was bisexual before this, it must have been a shock. As I see it, there are two ways to handle this. The first would be to talk with a licensed mental health professional and ask if that person can give you some insight into whether postpartum has caused your wife's behavior. None of this is your fault, and it may not be your wife's fault either.</p><p>The second way is to start interviewing lawyers who specialize in family law. While you can't force your wife to stay married to you, you can make sure your children spend as much time with their father as possible.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>For an excellent guide to becoming a better conversationalist and a more sociable person, order "How to Be Popular." Send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds), to: Dear Abby, Popularity Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: United Airlines' fight for supremacy at O'Hare is terrible for travelers
entries.summary: <p>Brace yourself if you're flying through O'Hare Airport this summer. Depending on the Federal Aviation Administration's decisions over the coming weeks, fewer choices, higher fares and more disruption are likely headed your way — and not entirely by accident. </p><p>The agency has been <a class="Link" href="https://www.reuters.com/world/us/faa-plans-reduce-flights-chicago-ohare-citing-boost-schedules-2026-02-27/" target="_blank" >forced to step in</a> ahead of the busy summer season to address the gap between scheduled flights and the capacity the airport is actually equipped to handle. For the millions of passengers who rely on O'Hare every year, the FAA’s decision will not be an abstraction. It could mean a missed connection, a canceled trip or a fare that suddenly costs twice what it did last year.</p><p>This crisis didn't happen on its own. It was triggered, and arguably even engineered, by United Airlines’ deliberate strategy to gain more control of the airport. Airlines have over-scheduled O'Hare for this upcoming summer, <a class="Link" href="https://www.cbsnews.com/chicago/news/faa-ohare-flight-reductions-united-american-airlines/" target="_blank" >projecting</a> more than 3,000 daily operations, up from around 2,680 during peak days last year. The FAA determined that the airport's infrastructure and air traffic control staffing cannot safely support that volume — and Chicago is all too familiar with why that can’t stand. During the 2025 government shutdown, controller shortages triggered ground stops and <a class="Link" href="https://abc7chicago.com/post/national-airspace-system-status-flight-cancellations-chicagos-ohare-midway-airports-begin-faa-reduces-capacity/18124735/" target="_blank" >dozens of daily cancellations</a> at O'Hare alone.</p><p>Knowing this, why are airlines scheduling so far beyond what the airport can handle?</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-270000" name="module-270000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Commentary bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Commentary</h2></div> </div><p>In the case of United Airlines, the answer is uncomfortable, but the airline's own leadership has made the strategy clear: Chaos is fine, as long as the fix hurts competitors more. United is planning 750 daily departures from O'Hare this summer, an <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagobusiness.com/airlines-airports/united-airlines-plans-record-number-ohare-flights" target="_blank" >increase of more than 30%</a>, according to Crain's Chicago Business. These <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagotribune.com/2026/03/04/editorial-united-american-airlines-ohare-airport-faa-summer-travel/" target="_blank" >aren’t in-demand routes</a> launched to help travelers. They are designed to exploit O'Hare's gate assignment policy, which allocates gates based solely on flights scheduled — a "use it or lose it" approach. </p><p>On a January earnings call, United CEO Scott Kirby <a class="Link" href="https://thepointsguy.com/news/united-american-airlines-chicago-ohare-hub-line-in-sand/" target="_blank" >declared</a>: "We're not going to allow them [American Airlines] to win a single gate at our expense in 2026." His chief financial officer <a class="Link" href="https://www.businessinsider.com/united-airlines-cfo-belittles-american-airlines-in-fight-for-chicago-2026-2" target="_blank" >called</a> American's presence at O'Hare temporary, predicting that "everybody is switching from American to United."</p><h3>Turf war</h3><p>This is not a passenger service strategy. This is a land grab. United Airlines is adding <a class="Link" href="https://www.travelweekly.com/Travel-News/Airline-News/United-new-routes-competitive-surge-OHare" target="_blank" >dozens</a> of low-demand flights, knowing that it can roll them back once the FAA mandates cuts and still come out ahead of other carriers forced to cancel their own valuable flights. The airline has also been buying up gates, including<a class="Link" href="https://simpleflying.com/why-united-paid-30m-for-gates-its-ceo-swore-he-didnt-want/" target="_blank" > paying $30 million for Spirit Airlines’ gates</a> that it previously claimed were not of interest. With its scheduling surge, United is burning through O'Hare's finite capacity not to better serve travelers but to crowd out rivals.</p><p>Why does this matter beyond a corporate turf war? Because O'Hare is one of the last major U.S. airports where two legacy carriers genuinely compete through dual hubs, and that competition directly benefits passengers. Fares stay lower. Service improves. Travelers have real choices.</p><p>American Airlines may not admit it, but with its comparatively smaller hub, it has invested in upgrading its Chicago product precisely because competition demands it.</p><p>The warning signs of a less competitive O'Hare are already visible. Southwest is already <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/03/13/southwest-airlines-ohare" target="_blank" >planning to exit the airport</a>. As United crowds out rivals, options disappear and travelers lose leverage. A United near-monopoly wouldn't just be bad for Chicago, it would be bad for the millions of connecting passengers who pass through O'Hare every year.</p><p>None of this lets the FAA and Congress off the hook. Flight caps are a band-aid, not a cure, and a resilient solution — at O’Hare, and beyond — means more work ahead. Our air traffic control system still runs on decades-old technology despite significant public investment over the past decade. Thousands of controller positions remain unfilled. Sens. Tammy Duckworth and Dick Durbin of Illinois have rightly <a class="Link" href="https://www.duckworth.senate.gov/news/press-releases/duckworth-durbin-call-for-greater-faa-investment-and-efficiency-at-chicago-ohare-airport" target="_blank" >urged</a> the FAA to fix O'Hare's staffing crisis beyond addressing flight schedules, but so far the urgency hasn't matched the rhetoric. U.S. travelers pay more than 20% of their ticket price in taxes and fees. They are entitled to a modern, functioning system in return.</p><h3>Testing travelers’ patience</h3><p>The FAA and Department of Transportation must examine whether United's scheduling practices constitute anti-competitive manipulation of gate allocation rules. Congress must treat air traffic control modernization as the national infrastructure emergency it is. Chicago and federal regulators must recognize that protecting O'Hare's dual-hub status is consumer protection, and ensure plans for flight cuts don’t ultimately reward anti-competitive behavior. And any flight reductions this summer must come with enforceable passenger protections: transparent rebooking, no fare-gouging and clear communication to affected travelers.</p><p>O'Hare's crisis is a microcosm of everything wrong in U.S. aviation: underinvestment in infrastructure, airlines that prioritize dominance over service, and regulators perpetually playing catch-up. Travelers have been patient through shutdowns, staffing crises and a capacity crunch that was foreseeable and deliberately manufactured.</p><p>The FAA has the authority to act. Congress has the tools. What has been missing for too long is the will to put travelers first. That's what every passenger flying through O'Hare this summer deserves.</p><p><i>Charlie Leocha is president and co-founder of the consumer advocacy group Travelers United.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-ce0000" name="module-ce0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Charlie Leocha

entries.title: Government shutdowns, long airport lines have become the new normal
entries.summary: <p>If you’ve ever traveled to France, chances are you’ve come up against this all-too-common phenomenon. You get to the train station and, without warning, your train is out of service. Or a restaurant is oddly closed during regular business hours.</p><p>"C’est la grève," you may hear from a local, accompanied by a shrug. It’s the strike.</p><p>There’s a joke in France about its time-honored tradition of labor protests: A tourist asks a waiter what the signs are about at a protest across the street. The waiter hardly looks up and simply says, "Thursday."</p><p>Strikes, protests, work stoppages and closures are so frequent in France, there are multiple apps and websites devoted to letting locals and tourists alike know what will be open and what will be closed, and those in the know check daily.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-d80000" name="module-d80000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnist </div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Columnist</div> </div><p>A strike today at schools in Dijon, Lyon and Rennes; an ongoing transport strike in Rouen; closures at the Louvre. <a class="Link" href="https://www.cestlagreve.fr/" target="_blank" >One site</a> promises: "Thanks to our service, unexpected problems are a thing of the past. You’ll no longer be stuck waiting for your train, and you can plan a backup solution!"</p><p>Americans may hear this and smugly scoff at a society that’s turned such dysfunction into a revered art form. But our pride is increasingly undeserved.</p><p>Last week I flew between Newark and Austin. As you may have seen on the news, the security lines were hours long and, in some cases, snaked out the airport doors and onto the street.</p><p>This wasn’t because of a protest or a strike, however, but yet another government shutdown, the third in just six months.</p><p>Starting in October last year and lasting for 43 days, we endured the longest shutdown in U.S. history due to an impasse over Affordable Care Act subsidies. Then, earlier this year, a partial shutdown over delays in a funding package linked to immigration. And now, going on more than a month, we’re in another partial shutdown over Department of Homeland Security funding.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/2026/03/16/travel/tsa-airport-delays-shutdown.html" target="_blank" >shutdown</a> has meant about 50,000 Transportation Security Administration officers have been working without pay. Many have picked up second jobs, while others are calling in sick and more than 300 have simply quit.</p><p>Acting Deputy TSA Administrator Adam Stahl says we’re rapidly approaching a breaking point:</p><p>“If the call rate does climb," <a class="Link" href="https://www.cbsnews.com/news/tsa-airports-could-shut-down-officer-call-out-rate-climbs/" target="_blank" >he said</a> of agents calling out of work, "there could be scenarios where we may have to shut down airports. This is a serious situation."</p><p>You’d think Congress would want to fix this quickly in an election year, but all evidence points to the contrary. Republicans and Democrats remain in a stalemate over DHS funding, with both parties blaming each other and using strikingly similar language to do so.</p><p>"Democrats need to end their political posturing, stop using our TSA agents as political pawns, and fully fund DHS," <a class="Link" href="https://www.cnn.com/2026/03/19/us/tsa-political-impacts" target="_blank" >said</a> Sen. Katie Britt, R-Ala.</p><p>Sen. Raphael Warnock, D-Ga., <a class="Link" href="https://www.youtube.com/shorts/nClkqs020bw" target="_blank" >said</a>, "I think it’s simply wrong for my Republican colleagues to use these hard-working Americans as leverage, as pawns, in what they are presenting as a false choice to the American people."</p><p>But while both parties finger-point, all the average American sees is a dysfunctional government getting more dysfunctional by the day, with basic pursuits — like air travel — feeling more like capricious and unreliable luxuries.</p><p>While inching through security in Austin, someone asked me what the lines were about. "The shutdown," I said. He didn’t even know we were in one again.</p><p>We deserve better from our government. But if this is just our new normal — where on any given day we’re left wondering what parts of our government are open and running — we may, like the French, need to get ourselves an app for that.</p><p><i>S.E. Cupp is the host of “S.E. Cupp Unfiltered” on CNN.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-8f0000" name="module-8f0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: S. E. Cupp

entries.title: Definition of a company man? Calbert Wright, who’s been working at Ford’s Chicago Heights plant since 1963
entries.summary: <p>He didn't think he'd stick around.</p><p>When Calbert Wright began work at the Ford Motor Company's Chicago Stamping Plant in Chicago Heights in 1963, the factory was a noisy, smelly, smoky hellscape with a leaky roof. </p><p>And it was hot: 100 degrees on the production line.</p><p>"Ooo," Wright said. "No fans. Water fountains were rare, very rare."</p><p>Plus Black workers such as himself were given the hardest duties.</p><p>"The first month, I didn't like it," remembers Wright, 85, "I said, 'I'm not going to stay here'. They had us stacking steel. We couldn't touch no presses. All we could do is stack stock. They were trying to work us like Hebrew slaves."</p><p>But stay he did.</p><p>When Wright began work at the age of 23 at Ford, John F. Kennedy was president. Henry Ford still ran the business — albeit Henry Ford II, grandson of the man who founded the automobile manufacturer in 1903.</p><p>That means Wright, who prowls the floor today checking that workers on the line have enough parts to keep the robots busy — and takes their place when they go on bathroom breaks — has worked for Ford a little more than half the 123 years since the company sold its first car, a two-cylinder, two-passenger Model A, in red, the only color available, for $850 to Ernest Pfennig, a dentist on Clybourn Avenue.</p><p>Wright had come up from Mississippi when he was 11, and his voice is rich with Southern drawl. He had an uncle at Ford's Torrence Avenue assembly plant, and got a job at Chicago Stamping.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-fe0000" name="image-fe0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9a360cb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4927x2765+0+260/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F96%2Fc8%2Fa503644245fa95201911bfcc85a9%2Fsteinberg-260322-11.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8615516/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4927x2765+0+260/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F96%2Fc8%2Fa503644245fa95201911bfcc85a9%2Fsteinberg-260322-11.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c813d2c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4927x2765+0+260/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F96%2Fc8%2Fa503644245fa95201911bfcc85a9%2Fsteinberg-260322-11.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Calbert Wright shows one of reference doors that workers use as a standard for a properly assembled door for the start of their shift at the Ford Chicago Stamping Plant in Chicago Heights Wednesday, March 18, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c813d2c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4927x2765+0+260/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F96%2Fc8%2Fa503644245fa95201911bfcc85a9%2Fsteinberg-260322-11.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ffbdbab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4927x2765+0+260/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F96%2Fc8%2Fa503644245fa95201911bfcc85a9%2Fsteinberg-260322-11.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c813d2c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4927x2765+0+260/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F96%2Fc8%2Fa503644245fa95201911bfcc85a9%2Fsteinberg-260322-11.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Calbert Wright shows one of reference doors that workers use as a standard for a properly assembled door for the start of their shift at the Ford Chicago Stamping Plant in Chicago Heights Wednesday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Gary Middendorf/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Why did he stay?</p><p>"There weren't jobs paying like this," he said, laughing: $1.40 an hour. "Big money."</p><p>He had a wife, Thelma — now married 65 years — and an infant son to consider. And things were changing.</p><p>"[Martin Luther] King, plus the union, made everybody be classified," Wright said. Conditions improved. He moved up from stacking steel. "That's why I stayed so long."</p><p>Wright's 63-year tenure isn't even the longest of Ford's 177,000 workers — that would be Art Porter, 86, who joined Chicago Stamping in 1961.</p><p>Their longevity is especially amazing when you realize how frequently workers change employers. According to the U.S. Department of Labor, the average worker at a private factory like Ford works 4.9 years before leaving.</p><p>Wright has put in a dozen times that, and seen many changes.</p><p>One of the biggest is automation. When robots were first introduced, in the 1970s, it wasn't clear whether they'd be a benefit.</p><p>"They were throwing parts all over," Wright said. "They were dangerous. They couldn't control it. They were putting welds in the wrong place, blowing holes."</p><h3>Better with robot help</h3><p>Gradually, the machines improved.</p><p>"They got it right now," Wright said. "They come out better with the robots. They put the welds in the same place. When they manpower with a gun, they put one here, one there."</p><p>Walking through the plant with Wright now, it's cool and almost quiet, except for the faint panoply of clanks and hisses. Only occasionally do you spy a person, shielded by machinery.</p><p>"Them robots came in and knocked all those people out," Wright said. "Each line would have 18 people, Now they got three. When I hired in, they had 6,500 people in this plant." </p><p>Now Chicago Stamping employs 1,100.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d50000" name="image-d50000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/72e5b4e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5452x3060+0+288/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F40%2F0d%2Fc174fec14f96ae1fa63996bb33dc%2Fsteinberg-260322-12.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/af06334/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5452x3060+0+288/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F40%2F0d%2Fc174fec14f96ae1fa63996bb33dc%2Fsteinberg-260322-12.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/229cf0b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5452x3060+0+288/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F40%2F0d%2Fc174fec14f96ae1fa63996bb33dc%2Fsteinberg-260322-12.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Calbert Wright stands by racks of doors on the assembly floor at the Ford Assembly Plant in Chicago Heights on Wednesday, March 18, 2026." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/229cf0b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5452x3060+0+288/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F40%2F0d%2Fc174fec14f96ae1fa63996bb33dc%2Fsteinberg-260322-12.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/64065d1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5452x3060+0+288/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F40%2F0d%2Fc174fec14f96ae1fa63996bb33dc%2Fsteinberg-260322-12.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/229cf0b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5452x3060+0+288/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F40%2F0d%2Fc174fec14f96ae1fa63996bb33dc%2Fsteinberg-260322-12.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Calbert Wright stands by racks of doors on the assembly floor at the Ford Chicago Stamping Plant in Chicago Heights on Wednesday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Gary Middendorf/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Wright works on one of four "door lines" — creating doors for Explorers and Lincoln Aviators.</p><p>"I'm a utility man," he said. "You go around the line, instruct people how to do the job. Keep the line running."</p><p>The best way to envision the factory floor is a series of stations, where robots work in a central core, moving like large, jerky birds, grabbing raw steel doors, sending off sheets of sparks as they apply welds affixing window channels, while several workers feed the parts to them. Two buttons control two lights at each station — yellow, signaling the worker needs more parts, and green, signaling repair is required. </p><p>A simple system, but one that has to be followed. This is where Wright comes in.</p><p>"You have to stay up on 'em, because, if they run out of parts, you'd think they'd hit that light?" said Wright. "You're on the line and everything is not cycling. You go up there, 'What's wrong?' [The worker says] "Well, I'm out of parts." [Wright says] "Why don't you hit the lights? The fork driver don't know."</p><p> </p><h3>‘The fun part’</h3><p>Is there a fun part to his job?</p><p>"The fun part..." said Wright. "I go home and say, 'I had a good day.' And got all these rejects, tuck 'em to the side, send them over to salvage. You had a good day."</p><p>He's referring to the detective work aspect to his work. It isn't enough to just pull a flawed part. You have to find out where the dent came from. What's causing it? </p><p>"You gotta find the source," Wright said. "You gotta solve the problem. Track it down from station to station."</p><p>It took three months to figure out what was putting dents into a certain lift gate.</p><p>"They finally caught up with where it was coming from," he said. A plate needed adjustment.</p><p>Machines don't stand up and admit doing something wrong — they're very much like people in that regard. Here Wright uses his experience to counteract the brashness of youngsters, who aren't averse to dropping a connection when called out on a mistake, instead of taking responsibility.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b30000" name="image-b30000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5104028/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4402x2471+0+232/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F29%2F2a%2Ff15916a1463eb3fde3dea663eace%2Fsteinberg-260322-09.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/482fe7e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4402x2471+0+232/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F29%2F2a%2Ff15916a1463eb3fde3dea663eace%2Fsteinberg-260322-09.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c28a068/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4402x2471+0+232/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F29%2F2a%2Ff15916a1463eb3fde3dea663eace%2Fsteinberg-260322-09.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A robotic arm moves a door on the assembly line at the Ford Chicago Stamping Plant in Chicago Heights on Wednesday, March 18, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c28a068/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4402x2471+0+232/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F29%2F2a%2Ff15916a1463eb3fde3dea663eace%2Fsteinberg-260322-09.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/407cd54/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4402x2471+0+232/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F29%2F2a%2Ff15916a1463eb3fde3dea663eace%2Fsteinberg-260322-09.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c28a068/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4402x2471+0+232/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F29%2F2a%2Ff15916a1463eb3fde3dea663eace%2Fsteinberg-260322-09.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A robotic arm moves a door on the assembly line at the Ford Chicago Stamping Plant in Chicago Heights on Wednesday. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Gary Middendorf/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>"You have to feel somebody out," Wright said. "Don't tell me your daddy works out front. That don't get you any special privileges on the floor. You have to carry your weight like everybody else."</p><p>Otherwise, the challenge is, "To come in here, and try to run some good parts, and try to get everybody to line up on the same page. A good group will let you know there's something wrong. They love this job."</p><h3>60 years, same workplace</h3><p>Six decades in one factory — what stands out?</p><p>Shortly after he joined, Wright said, a new hire stepped in front of a fork lift carrying a roll of steel — the driver hit the brakes, the steel rolled off the forks and killed him on the spot. He'd been there three days. That taught Wright a valuable lesson.</p><p>"I always been careful when I walk down the aisle," he said. He is surprisingly fit for a man in his mid-80s.</p><p>"I used to go to the gym all the time, LA Fitness, me and my grandson," said Wright.</p><p>His grandson worked in Chicago Stamping for a while, but was killed in a motorcycle accident. "I told him that bike was too fast for him," said Wright, who has seen his share of tragedy. His son also died. But his daughter, Libra, works for the Internal Revenue Service, having gone to Governors State University with lots of support from Ford. "They gave her a lot of money," he said.</p><p>"I said, 'Do better than I did,'" Wright remembered. "Education will get you a chance for advancement."</p><p>He is proud of his two grandchildren, one in the Navy.</p><p>There is also a pride that comes from working for one place so long. The Explorers, for instance.</p><p>"I said, 'This car come out of our plant.' It's beautiful. My daughter got one already."</p><p></p><p>Why does Wright stay? He could be living the life of leisure.</p><p>"To do something, get out of the house," he said. "Otherwise I'd go batty.</p><p>How does he know? Five years ago he took his five weeks of vacation in a single block.</p><p>"I tried it," Wright said. He didn't like it.</p><p>"Sit around, do nothing, the same thing on TV," he scoffed. "Reruns. I get tired of looking at reruns."</p><p>Ford speaks of their employees as family, and sometimes that is literally true.</p><p>"This happens to be my godfather," said Shanetta Gibbs-Cowgur, the United Auto Workers union employee support services programs representative for the plant, whose father Charles worked at Ford Stamping for 31 years. "Before I hired in, he and my dad were friends. It was guys like him who took the younger generation under their wings and showed them about safety, being able to maneuver from Job to job safely."</p><p>Wright lives in Matteson and goes to casinos for fun.</p><p>Looking back over his life, he has no regrets.</p><p>"I could have done a lot of things different," Wright said. "But you can't go back and dream of what you should or could have did. I've been fortunate."</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2024/02/27/ford-assembly-plant-chicago-torrence-avenue-100-years-model-t-taurus-explorer" >A century of Ford cars made at Torrence Avenue</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Neil Steinberg

entries.title: Chicago Park District pays more than $2 million to ‘sexually exploited’ lifeguard
entries.summary: <p>The notoriously toxic workplace culture that once festered at Oak Street Beach led the Chicago Park District to pay more than $2 million to a female former lifeguard there — bringing the total legal tab for the sexual-misconduct scandal at the city’s public beaches and pools to more than $8.7 million.</p><p>The alleged misconduct in the case included “grooming, exploitation and sexual assault” by Park District supervisors that began when the plaintiff was still a “minor,” according to court records.</p><p>The recent settlement with the woman, who worked at the popular beach near downtown, marks the seventh legal payout to a former lifeguard since WBEZ first reported on <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/politics/2021/04/27/chicago-park-district-investigating-widespread-sex-abuse-claims" target="_blank" ><u>widespread allegations of sexual abuse</u></a>, assault and harassment in the Park District’s Aquatics Department five years ago.</p><p>Asked about the latest settlement with an unidentified “Jane Doe” plaintiff, a Park District spokeswoman said the agency “considers multiple factors before reaching a settlement agreement and believes the payment is fair and in the best interest of the District.”</p><p>The spokeswoman also said there were no pending court cases and no other unresolved out-of-court claims related to the scandal.</p><p>The total payout in the recently settled case was $2.15 million, according to Park District board meeting records.</p><p>The Chicago law firm of Romanucci & Blandin LLC represented the plaintiff in the case, but a spokeswoman for the firm said the terms of the agreement with the Park District prohibited lawyers from commenting.</p><p>The same firm had won the biggest of the seven settlements related to the lifeguard-abuse scandal for another client. That litigation ended two years ago with the Park District <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/politics/2024/07/30/abused-chicago-lifeguards-receive-more-than-6-5-million-in-legal-settlements" target="_blank" ><u>paying $4 million</u></a> to a woman who alleged abuse by a lifeguard supervisor at Humboldt Park.</p><p>In the case at Oak Street Beach, Cook County Circuit Court records show the plaintiff worked there every summer from 2012 through 2018 and filed the lawsuit in October 2024 against the Park District and its former CEO Michael Kelly.</p><p>The complaint alleged that Kelly and other officials “allowed and concealed a pervasive institutional culture of sexual misconduct directed against female [Park District] lifeguards.”</p><p>The plaintiff became “a victim of [the Park District’s] indifference” — and of senior lifeguards at the iconic beach on Lake Michigan in Chicago’s Gold Coast, according to court records.</p><p>The former lifeguard alleged that the Park District’s leadership “failed to investigate reported sexual abuse at Oak Street Beach” and did not fire or otherwise discipline supervisors who committed sexual misconduct against female employees.</p><p>The case was closed in January, court records show, after the Park District’s board approved the settlement agreement with no dissenting votes. Video from the meeting shows the Park District commissioners did not discuss the matter publicly before approving the settlement.</p><p>The agreement brings the total for the seven payouts to former lifeguards to $8,727,250, according to Park District records obtained by WBEZ.</p><p>In addition to the $4 million case — believed to be the biggest payout by the Park District for any legal matter since at least 2001 — the agency agreed to settlements with two other former Aquatics Department employees in 2024. Those settlements were for $400,000 and $275,000.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/politics/2023/09/27/chicago-park-districts-big-payouts-to-three-lifeguards" target="_blank" ><u>first three claims</u></a> related to the scandal ended with a settlement for $575,000 in 2022 and payouts of $977,250 and $350,000 in 2023, Park District records show.</p><p>The scandal prompted the <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/politics/2021/12/07/chicago-parks-lifeguard-abuse-investigation-timeline" target="_blank" ><u>resignations of Kelly</u></a>, Park District Inspector General <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/wbez-investigations/2021/09/14/top-watchdog-over-chicago-lifeguard-investigation-resigns" target="_blank" ><u>Elaine Little</u></a>, other high-ranking agency officials and Park District Board President <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/wbez-investigations/2022/06/01/the-former-chicago-parks-leader-blamed-political-pressure-for-her-exit" target="_blank" ><u>Avis LaVelle</u></a>, with then-Mayor Lori Lightfoot ordering an outside-counsel investigation into the matter.</p><p>The park district watchdog’s office opened its probe in 2020, when two young female former lifeguards sent detailed letters to Lightfoot and Kelly. But the matter remained out of public view for more than a year, until WBEZ reported on what already was a <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/stories/chicago-park-district-investigating-widespread-sex-abuse-claims/dbd56a93-9c80-403d-bb0b-c26ea8b29f24" target="_blank" ><u>“broad investigation”</u></a> in April 2021.</p><p>After the story broke, many former lifeguards came forward and said the <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/politics/2021/06/17/female-ex-lifeguards-describe-deacdes-of-sexual-abuse-at-chicagos-beaches-and-pools" target="_blank" ><u>sexual misconduct was endemic</u></a> at the beaches and pools for generations, with officials accused of failing to follow through on their zero-tolerance promises.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/stories/chicago-park-district-suspends-two-lifeguard-managers/90fdf9c2-b1a2-4e50-bcb8-39a6e3ae340e" target="_blank" ><u>Kelly defended his handling</u></a> of the matter, but <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/politics/2021/08/23/whistleblower-chicago-mayor-should-fire-parks-leaders" target="_blank" ><u>former lifeguards told WBEZ</u></a> they were disappointed by the official response. City Council members <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/politics/2021/10/04/lifeguard-abuse-prompts-calls-for-chicago-parks-ceo-to-resign" target="_blank" ><u>raised concerns</u></a>, and Lightfoot eventually <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/stories/lightfoot-parks-chief-should-lose-job-over-lifeguard-sex-abuse-scandal/c9692289-cb0c-409c-a638-942b729aaeff" target="_blank" ><u>forced Kelly to resign</u></a>.</p><p>The outside counsel’s investigation for the Park District concluded that Kelly knew about the explosive allegations far longer than previously revealed and did nothing to investigate the complaints immediately, contrary to his public assertions.</p><p>The Park District inspector general’s office ultimately found <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/stories/chicago-parks-watchdog-reveals-lifeguard-abuse-findings/d10d4bf4-8e0e-42fc-bfc2-904135d95814" target="_blank" ><u>proof to back up 29 allegations</u></a> against employees in the Aquatics Department, concluding that “bullying, harassing and sexual misconduct flourished and went unchallenged.”</p><p>Officials promised reforms to avoid a repeat of the scandal, and the Park District has <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagoparkdistrict.com/be-lifeguard" target="_blank" ><u>begun recruiting </u></a>lifeguards for the 2026 season. The next swim test for prospective lifeguards is March 28 at Whitney Young High School.</p><p>The Park District is offering $20.50 an hour for lifeguarding this summer, according to a help-wanted ad.</p><p><i>Dan Mihalopoulos is an investigative reporter for WBEZ.</i><br></p>
entries.author: Dan Mihalopoulos | WBEZ

entries.title: Chicago Mayor Brandon Johnson's budget director got illegal property tax breaks for five years
entries.summary: <p>For the past five years, Mayor Brandon Johnson’s budget director has illegally gotten property tax breaks on a South Loop condo she owns but hasn’t lived in since 2019.</p><p>Annette Guzman got those tax breaks from her former boss, Cook County Assessor Fritz Kaegi. They've saved her $3,434 in taxes on a condo she called an investment property on the required financial disclosure statement she filed with the Cook County clerk a year ago.</p><p>Guzman and Kaegi say that, until asked by a Chicago Sun-Times reporter about the tax breaks, they were unaware she was violating state law by collecting the homeowner exemption on her investment property and also her Bronzeville home.</p><p>Illinois law allows homeowners one such exemption every year and requires them to live in the home.</p><p>Now, Kaegi’s staff has canceled the homeowner exemption on the investment property and ordered Guzman to repay $2,071.89 in tax breaks she got — not what she wrongfully failed to pay for the past five years but just for the past three years. Kaegi also waived any interest payments.</p><p>Guzman repaid the money Tuesday, according to Kaegi’s office.</p><p>“I paid what they told me to pay,” Guzman says when asked why she wasn’t ordered to repay, with interest, what she saved from each of the five illegal tax breaks she's gotten since 2020.</p><p>Kaegi’s lawyer Christina Lynch says the assessor's erroneous exemption team is now reviewing Guzman’s illegal tax breaks to determine whether she should also repay the tax breaks for the earlier two years, when she was living with her then-husband in his Northwest Side home.</p><p>After she moved out of the condo, Guzman says she went to Kaegi’s office in 2021 — the assessor says it was in 2020, during the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic — asking to cancel the homeowner tax exemption on her South Loop condo.</p><p>Lynch says “we never processed it.” </p><p>So Guzman kept getting the homeowner exemption on her South Loop condo as well as the Bronzeville condo, where she has lived for the past five years.</p><p>“This was an assessor error, and we have waived interest,” Lynch says.</p><p>Guzman says she didn’t know the assessor kept giving her tax breaks on the South Loop condo for two reasons: Her lender paid the property taxes with money in her escrow account, and she never changed the mailing address on those tax bills, which are still mailed to her ex-husband’s house, four years after they divorced.</p><p>Guzman, who has a law degree from the University of Chicago, bought the South Loop condo in 2007. She was living there in 2019 while she was Kaegi’s chief administrative officer, overseeing thousands of homeowner exemptions for Cook County property owners.</p><p>She says she moved out of that condo when she married Martin Guzman in September 2019, moving into his house, which also receives a homeowner exemption.</p><p>Guzman, 45, left Kaegi’s staff in January 2020 and went to work as the budget director for Cook County Board President Toni Preckwinkle. Johnson hired her as city budget director in May 2023.</p><p>Once Guzman moved out of her condo and into her husband’s home, she no longer was entitled to receive a homeowner exemption on her condo, under state law.</p><p>A year after the marriage, Guzman moved out of her husband’s home, filing for divorce in February 2021, when she disclosed she was living in a Loop apartment a few blocks north of her office as the Cook County budget director, court records show.</p><p>As the divorce was pending, Guzman refinanced her South Loop condo in March 2021, according to the mortgage filed with the Cook County clerk. She declared that the unit in the 1800 block of South State Street wasn’t owner-occupied, making it ineligible for a homeowner exemption.</p><p>“It was a rental property after I moved out from my husband’s home,” Guzman says.</p><p>She also bought a second condo in the 4000 block of South Ellis Avenue, where she now lives.</p><p>“When I purchased my home that I live in now, I went to the assessor’s office and asked to remove the homeowner’s exemption” from the South Loop condo, Guzman says.</p><p>Six months later, Cook County officials mailed the 2020 tax bill on Guzman’s South Loop condo to her husband’s address. The bill included a homeowner exemption, which cut her tax bill by $691.10. It was her first erroneous tax break since she acknowledged she moved out of the unit in 2019.</p><p>Since Guzman bought her Bronzeville condo in March 2021, she has gotten four annual tax bills on that unit and four annual bills on the South Loop condo. Each of those bills contained homeowner exemptions, cutting the property taxes on the two properties at the same time, a violation of the law that limits those exemptions to one property each year.</p><p>Over the past four years, Guzman saved a total of $2,743.49 from the homeowner exemptions on her Bronzeville home and $2,743.49 in erroneous exemptions on her South Loop home.</p><p>Altogether, records show, she got $3,434.59 in erroneous exemptions on the South Loop condo over the past five years, yet the assessor’s office told her to repay only $2,071.89. It's unclear if the assessor will order her to repay the $1,362.70 in tax breaks she got while she wasn't living in the South Loop condo.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2024/11/15/senior-freeze-property-tax-fritz-kaegi-cook-county-assessor" >Fritz Kaegi OK’d tax breaks for ‘low-income’ seniors, now demands proof they qualify amid Sun-Times probe</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2021/8/6/22611906/walter-burnett-darlena-williams-burnett-homeowner-property-tax-exemption" >Ald. Walter Burnett, wife wrongly claimed 2 homeowner tax exemptions for 8 years</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/01/17/senior-freeze-property-tax-exemption-fritz-kaegi-armando-saleh-cook-county-assessor-jill-fitzgerald" >City Hall lobbyist pays $96,000 for tax breaks Sun-Times showed he never should have been given</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Tim Novak

entries.title: Woman charged with attempted murder after hitting man with car in Roseland, police say
entries.summary: <p>A woman was charged after she was alleged to have hit a man with her vehicle in Roseland last month, according to Chicago police.</p><p>Ashanti Harvey, 24, faces a count for attempted first-degree murder and aggravated battery, police said in a statement. She was arrested Thursday.</p><p>Harvey is alleged to have hit a 25-year-old man with her vehicle in the 11100 block of South State Street at 3:40 p.m. Feb. 3, police said. </p><p>Harvey is due in court Friday.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

held at The Brew coffee shop located at 7101 S. Yates Blvd. on the South Side, Tuesday, Jan. 13, 2026. | Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wttkdlt-l-d/" target="_blank" >Johnson fires deputy mayor of community safety amid toxic workplace allegations</a></h3><h3></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wttkdlt-l-k/" target="_blank" >Fran Spielman</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wttkdlt-l-u/" target="_blank" >Sophie Sherry</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wttkdlt-l-o/" target="_blank" >Tim Novak</a></h5><p><b>Abrupt firing</b>: Deputy Mayor of Community Safety Garien Gatewood was abruptly fired Thursday amid allegations of a hostile work environment and of self-promotion. Gatewood was a leader of the community safety plan that Mayor Brandon Johnson credits for historic drops in violent crime. First Deputy Jason Sanford will serve in the interim role.</p><p><b>'Deteriorating rapidly'</b>: The mayor’s office released a statement confirming the change but saying little else, per its policy of not discussing personnel issues. Sources close to the mayor’s senior staff said the environment around Gatewood’s team was "deteriorating rapidly," and that multiple team members made reports of "hostile gestures" toward women "not aggressively but in a way that made them uncomfortable." Gatewood countered that he created a "welcoming and open" work environment.</p><p><b>More on Johnson personnel</b>: For the past five years, budget director Annette Guzman has illegally gotten <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wttkdlt-jksldvkt-b/" target="_blank" >$3,434 in property tax breaks </a>on a South Loop condo she owns but hasn’t lived in since 2019. Guzman called the condo an investment property on a financial disclosure statement. She and Cook County Assessor Fritz Kaegi, her ex-boss who gave her the breaks, said they were unaware she was violating state law by collecting the homeowner exemption on both her investment property and her Bronzeville home.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wttkdlt-l-d/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>LIFEGUARD LAWSUIT ⚖️</h4><div Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wttkdlt-l-p/" target="_blank" >Chicago Park District pays more than $2 million to ‘sexually exploited’ lifeguard</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wttkdlt-l-x/" target="_blank" >Dan Mihalopoulos</a></h5><p><b>Legal tab rises</b>: The notoriously toxic workplace culture that once festered at Oak Street Beach has led the Chicago Park District to pay more than $2 million to a female former lifeguard — bringing the total legal payout for the sexual misconduct scandal at the city’s public beaches and pools to more than $8.7 million.</p><p><b>Seventh payout</b>: The recent settlement marks the seventh legal payout to a former lifeguard since WBEZ first reported on widespread allegations of sexual abuse, assault and harassment in the district’s Aquatics Department five years ago. Cook County Circuit Court records show the plaintiff worked there every summer from 2012 through 2018 and filed the lawsuit in October 2024 against the Park District and its former CEO Michael Kelly.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Alleged misconduct in the case included "grooming, exploitation and sexual assault" by Park District supervisors that began when the plaintiff was a minor, according to court records. The suit alleged Kelly and other officials "allowed and concealed a pervasive institutional culture of sexual misconduct directed against" female lifeguards. It alleged district leadership "failed to investigate reported sexual abuse at Oak Street Beach" and did not fire or discipline supervisors who committed sexual misconduct.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wttkdlt-l-p/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>EDUCATION 📚</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-770000" name="image-770000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wttkdlt-l-z/" target="_blank" >CPS revokes Aspira’s ability to operate charter schools in Chicago in rare move</a></h3><h3></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wttkdlt-l-v/" target="_blank" >Sarah Karp</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wttkdlt-l-e/" target="_blank" >Emmanuel Camarillo</a></h5><p><b>Contract ends:</b> The Chicago Board of Education voted unanimously Thursday to end its contract with the financially troubled Aspira charter network — the only time the school district has revoked a charter in the middle of the school year. The charter will officially become inactive April 4 and students will not be able to enroll at an Aspira school for next year.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Aspira’s two high schools, which started the school year with about 600 students, are being emptied out. The organization's leaders said it <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wttkdlt-jksldvkt-g/" target="_blank" >didn’t have enough money</a> to continue operating through the end of the year, and the school district said state law forbade it from <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wttkdlt-jksldvkt-w/" target="_blank" >continuing to float money to Aspira</a>. Board members didn't comment before cancelling the contract, but in the past, several said Aspira’s <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wttkdlt-jksldvkt-yd/" target="_blank" >poor financial management</a> necessitated the closure.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wttkdlt-l-z/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>MORE href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wttkdlt-l-jt/" target="_blank" >New owner of the Hideout plans to continue venue’s legacy as a cultural institution and community hub</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wttkdlt-l-ji/" target="_blank" >Selena Fragassi</a></h5><p><b>Changing of the guard</b>: For the first time in 30 years, the Hideout has a new owner. Teri O’Brien — a performing artist and former employee of the West Town concert venue — is taking the reins from husband-and-wife team Tim and Katie Tuten and brothers Mike and Jim Hinchsliff, the venue announced Thursday on its website.</p><p><b>Cultural institution</b>: The Tutens and the Hinchsliffs first bought the converted century-old house along the North Branch of the Chicago River in 1996. Over the years, they’ve turned it into a cultural institution and community hub that was recognized as a "best" music venue by publications including Rolling Stone and USA Today, and was a pit stop for figures like Anthony Bourdain.</p><p><b>Key quote</b>: "The Hideout will stay the Hideout," Tim Tuten said in a statement. "It will remain independent, creative and rooted in the community that built it. Teri knows our room, the people and our history, and she has the heart to carry it forward.” O'Brien said she was looking into building partnerships and alliances with the city and nonprofit organizations, and would work with staff to gather ideas for the venue's sustainability.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wttkdlt-l-jt/" target="_blank" ><b>READ Museum in Little Italy on the Near West Side, Thursday, March 19, 2026. | Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wttkdlt-l-tj/" target="_blank" >Home for Good event promotes second chances for formerly incarcerated</a></h3><h3></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wttkdlt-l-tt/" target="_blank" >Araceli Gómez-Aldana</a></h5><p>When Andre Ruddock was born, doctors told his family they didn’t think he would live. He had health problems because his mother was addicted to heroin when she was pregnant.</p><p>Ruddock doesn’t see his upbringing as a sad story, but he does think he became a product of the negative parts of his environment. Ruddock was incarcerated at age 16 and spent 28 years in prison. As his release date approached after spending decades in prison, he faced the difficult reality of having no place to live.</p><p>When he was released, Ruddock was told he had been approved for help through the Illinois Housing Development Authority’s reentry rental assistance program, upon which the Home for Good Coalition aims to build. "I grew up in prison, so I’m 44 years old now. That’s my first time in the world as an adult ... To have my own set of keys was a big deal."</p><p>That's why Ruddock is advocating for legislation proposed by Home for Good to increase housing and support services for people like him returning from prison. The group is made up of Illinois advocacy organizations.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wttkdlt-l-tj/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p>Earlier, we asked you: <u>Who is your dream festival headliner, either an act you've seen or one you think would rule?</u></p><p>Here’s some of what you said…</p><p>“Coldplay; let’s do the kiss cam Chi-town style." — <i>Rich Barber</i></p><p>“Terrapin Flyer and Dark Star Orchestra!" — <i>Chris Tauber</i></p><p>“Jack White at Lolla circa 2013 was by far the best festival show I’ve ever seen." — <i>Angela Brooks</i></p><p>“Daft Punk would be legendary." — <i>Edward Scott Rosson</i></p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by:</b> Phyllis Cha<br><b>Editor:</b> Eydie Cubarrubia</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. Become a member to make stories like these free and available to everyone. Learn more at </i><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-th/" target="_blank" ><i>suntimes.com/member</i></a><i>.</i></p>
entries.author: Phyllis Cha

entries.title: Woman hospitalized after Roseland stabbing
entries.summary: <p>A woman was hospitalized after being stabbed by another woman in Roseland early Friday morning in what appears to be a domestic incident, according to Chicago police.</p><p>The woman, 22, was arguing with another woman in the 11500 block of South Perry Avenue when she stabbed the 22-year-old in the thigh and forearm, police said. She was taken to Christ Medical Center in serious condition, police said.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Chicago set for three days of 60+ degree temps
entries.summary: <p>Chicago is slated for a dose of spring weather this weekend, though temperatures may still dip overnight into the 30s.</p><p>Friday will bring sunny skies and a high temperature expected near 70 degrees, with a high of 65 Saturday and 60 on Sunday, according to the National Weather Service. </p><p>Saturday's low of 49 degrees will be the only nightly reprieve Chicagoans get, as temperatures are expected to drop to 33 Sunday night after dropping to 39 Friday night, forecasters say.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop="">
